entry.title: SAP Cloud Integration – Getting started with Google Pub/Sub Adapter
entry.content:
Google Pub/Sub is a reliable messaging tool that lets your systems talk to each other securely using 'topics.' It’s great for sharing data in real-time across different cloud platforms. With the Google Pub/Sub adapter in SAP CPI, you can easily send or receive messages, making it much simpler to connect your SAP processes with Google Cloud 

The Google Pub/Sub Adapter is available in both sender and receiver modes to streamline message-based workflows and comes in two variants — REST and gRPC. 

Key Features 

  • Publish single messages (Basic) or multiple messages (Advanced) at once. 
  • Use gRPC for real-time performance or REST for polling at defined intervals. 
  • Google Pub/Sub accepts only Base64-encoded data, and the adapter can handle encoding automatically. 
  • Provides multiple post-processing options: Acknowledge after Successful Processing, Acknowledge Immediately, and Do not Acknowledge.  
  • Supports Authentication via OAuth2 service account authentication and Workload Identity Federation.  

Practical Use Case Walkthrough 
 
In this section, we will demonstrate how to send SalesOrder data to a topic using the Google Pub/Sub Receiver adapter and then read the message again with the Google Pub/Sub Sender adapter, using the REST protocol for messaging. 

Publish a Message using Google PubSub Receiver Adapter 

Prepare Payload in Content Modifier  
 
For the demonstration, we hardcode the SalesOrder data into the message body. In real time, you can dynamically store the payload from the source system. 

Configure the Google Pub/Sub Receiver Adapter 

Connection tab: 

Parameter  

Description  

Address  

Specify the hostname to connect Google Pub/Sub. 

Authentication Type  

Select the method for authentication to Google Pub/Sub. 

  • OAuth2 Service Account 
  • Workload Identity Federation  

OAuth2 Token URL  

Specify the OAuth2 Token URL which identifies as the authorization server for producing a JWT token internally.  

Client Email  

Specify Google Service Account Client Email. 

Private Key Alias  

Specify the Google Service Account Private Key for authentication.  

Scope  

Specify the scope of the connection to Google Pub/Sub service. 

Reuse Connection  

Enable the reuse of connection objects from the internal connection pool which in turn improves the network turnaround time for multiple communications to the same end point. 

Connection Timeout (in ms)  

Specify the maximum waiting time (in milliseconds) for the connection to be established with Google Pub/Sub service.  

Response Timeout (in ms)  

Specify the maximum waiting time (in milliseconds) for a response message to be received with Google Pub/Sub service.   

Publish Message using Google Pub/Sub Adapter 

Now let’s navigate to the Processing tab, select the Entity as Topic, and select the operation as Publish Message for the use case. 

Also, provide the project ID and topic ID created on the Google Pub/Sub server. 

Processing Tab: 

Parameter 

Description 

Entity  

Select the entity for message processing:  

  • Topics 
  • Subscriptions  

Operation  

Select the operation to be performed.  

  • Publish Message
  • List  

Project ID  

Specify the Google Pub/Sub project ID.  

Topic ID  

Specify the Topic ID created on Google Pub/Sub service 

Operation Type  

Select the type of operation  

  • Basic 
  • Advanced  

Encode Data  

Select the checkbox to encode data to Base64 

Ordering key  

Specify the ordering key for this message.   

Message Attributes  

Specify multiple message attributes as key value pairs. 

Once you run the iflow, you can see the message processed successfully. 

In addition, you can see that the Sales Order message was successfully published to the Google Pub/Sub service. 

Read a Message using Google Pub/Sub Sender Adapter 

The Google Pub/Sub Sender Adapter is available in gRPC and REST formats. For this demonstration, we're using the REST variant. 

Processing Tab: 

In the Google Pub/Sub Sender Adapter, provide the Subscription ID and Project ID from which the message will be read on the Google Pub/Sub Server.  

You can also select the post-processing action once the message has been polled from the Google Pub/Sub Server. 

Once the iflow is successfully deployed, we can check it in the monitoring page. In response, we can see the message body. 

Conclusion: 

The Google Pub/Sub adapter in SAP CPI brings integrations to life by allowing real-time, event-driven communication between SAP and Google Cloud. It enables CPI to easily publish and receive messages at scale, making integrations faster, more responsive, and loosely linked. The adapter's cloud-native design enables the creation of modern, durable integrations that can quickly change with business needs. 

Quick Links: 

  • For information about Google Pub/Sub Sender adapter configuration, see  SAP Help | Google Pub/Sub Sender Adapter.  
  • For information about Google Pub/Sub Receiver adapter configuration, see  SAP Help | Google Pub/Sub Receiver Adapter.  
  • For detailed information about the Google Pub/Sub adapter, see Google Pub/Sub Adapter Guide 

The next blog will take this further with an exciting new use case — so stay tuned for more insights and updates!

entry.title: Use SAP Integration Suite to connect SAP and AWS services
entry.content:
Bridging the gap between SAP and 3rd party systems 

Everyone already knows that SAP Integration Suite is the election tool to connect your SAP services. But did you know that it is also your tool to integrate Everything, Everywhere, All in One Place? (yes, almost like the Oscar award winning movie 🏆)

Use SAP Integration Suite to connect SAP and AWS services is a new official SAP Discovery Center Mission that focus on showcasing how easy it is to integrate not only SAP systems but also non-SAP systems using SAP Integration Suite. 

For this mission, we decided to use the AWS platform as the example of third-party cloud services provider, but the same rational with this set of best practices and capabilities can be used to integrate with other cloud providers. By following the cards provided in the mission, you will see step-by-step how you can start to integrate an application developed with SAP BTP development tools with AWS services, such as S3 for image storage and Rekognition for image identification.
You can even use a SAP BTP Trial account to perform the SAP BTP parts of the mission, even though you will always still require also an AWS account to connect with the S3 and Rekognition services (and IAM for user and roles management on the AWS side). Either if you are a technical user or more of a business user interested in rapid application development, cloud integration strategies and/or AI-powered business solution, this mission is for you!

The following architecture shows the big picture of what the final result of the mission should look like, seamlessly combining the best of both SAP BTP and AWS platforms:

 

Use Case Description

The use case followed trough out the mission focuses in enabling a Supplier to donate surplus food ingredients through an AI-powered mobile and web application built using SAP Build Apps, a low-code/no-code solution within SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP), seamlessly integrated with AWS capabilities for storage and artificial intelligence–based image recognition. For the integration part, you will leverage and combine different integration capabilities that are part of the SAP Integration Suite. The intended outcome of the use case is to minimize food waste while simplifying the donation process and facilitating redistribution to organizations in need.

Solution Components:

  • SAP Build Apps: The low-code/no-code application development service used to create the application (frontend and logic) where the Supplier can donate the surplus ingredients by taking or uploading photos 
  • SAP Integration Suite: The secure and centralized integration management service for all your integrations, making them easier to manage, monitor and troubleshoot
  • Amazon S3: The Amazon service for simplified and cost-effective image storage capabilities
  • Amazon Rekognition: The Amazon service for AI-powered identification and categorization, in this case used to identify the ingredients in the images taken or uploaded from the application to a S3 bucket

The Use Case Flow:

  1. A Supplier captures or uploads an image of surplus ingredients through the mobile/web app (developed with SAP Build Apps)
  2. The image is securely stored in Amazon S3 via SAP Integration Suite (using Cloud Integration, Pre-built Open Connector and API Management capabilities)
  3. Also via SAP Integration Suite, the app then calls Amazon Rekognition to automatically identify and categorize the ingredient present in the image (using Cloud Integration, Custom Open Connector and API Management capabilities)
  4. The results are sent back to the app and ready for further processing

  

Learning Outcomes

By completing this Discovery Center Mission, you'll gain practical experience in:

  • Building low-code applications with SAP Build Apps
  • Configuring SAP Integration Suite for AWS connectivity
  • Implementing secure cloud-to-cloud integrations
  • Leveraging AI services for business process automation
  • Managing integration flows and monitoring capabilities

Getting Started

Once you open and start the Mission, you will see in the Project Board the different cards that will guide you step-by-step, divided in 7 columns.

Make sure you fullfill all the requirements indicated in the SET UP and PREPARE columns. After that, you can start with the EXECUTE columns. You will first explore the Open Connectors capability of SAP Integration Suite (in the Execute - OpenConnectors), followed by the Cloud Integration and API Management capabilities of SAP Integration Suite (in the Execute - Security Material and Integration Flow) and lastly you will get to visually create a simple app screen with SAP Build Apps (in Execute - Build app) .
After finishing the mission, don't forget to mark all the cards as completed and we encourage you to leave your feedback and share your experiences with the rest of the SAP Community. 

And now, for those who saw the movie Everything Everywhere All at Once, DON'T "Just be a rock" and start the mission here!  ->  SAP Discovery Center Missions - Use SAP Integration Suite to connect SAP and AWS services

entry.title: Connecting SAP Sales Cloud V2 to a Standalone SAP Analytics Cloud - A Practical Alternative
entry.content:
The Customer Challenge

A customer approached me with what seemed like a straightforward request: "We want to analyze our SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 data in SAP Analytics Cloud."

Their situation was common for mid-sized companies adopting SAP's CX portfolio:

  • SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 was live and running well
  • SAP Analytics Cloud was licensed separately (not the embedded version)
  • No SAP BTP subscription yet, it wasn't part of the initial scope
  • No SAP Integration Suite same reason
  • No SAP Datasphere, they wanted to avoid another data platform

The goal was simple: import Accounts, Contacts, and Opportunities into SAC for custom dashboards that combined CX data with data from other sources

What SAP Officially Recommends

Before building anything custom, I researched SAP's official guidance. The recommended architecture for Sales Cloud V2 analytics integration involves:

  1. SAP Datasphere as a central data layer
  2. SAP Integration Suite for event-driven data replication
  3. SAP Advanced Event Mesh (optional but recommended) for resilience

SAP published a detailed two-part blog series on this: Event-Driven Data Integration from SAP Sales and Service Cloud V2 to SAP Datasphere

This architecture is elegant and production-grade. It's also significant additional investment for a customer who just wants to run a few reports.

Cost Implications

The official path requires more than you might expect:

ComponentStatus for this Customer
SAP DatasphereNot licensed (€€€/month)
SAP Integration SuiteNot licensed (€€/month)
SAP BTP subscriptionNot available
Implementation effortSignificant (weeks)


For a customer with ~24,000 accounts who wants three dashboards, this felt like using a sledgehammer to crack a nut.

The Obvious First Attempt: SAC's Built-in Connectors

SAC has connectors for various SAP sources. Surely there's something for Sales Cloud?

Attempt 1: The "SAP Cloud for Customer" Connector

SAC includes a dedicated connector called "SAP Cloud for Customer" that works beautifully with the older C4C (Cloud for Customer) system. It connects to OData services like:

https://<tenant>/sap/c4c/odata/v1/c4codataapi/AccountCollection

The problem: Sales Cloud V2 is not C4C. So the OData connector won’t work:

Attempt 2: The Generic OData Connector

SAC also has a generic "OData Services" connector. Maybe that works?

The problem: Sales Cloud V2 doesn't expose OData endpoints. The APIs on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub are REST, not OData.

GET /sap/c4c/api/v1/account-service/accounts ← REST, not OData
GET /sap/c4c/api/v1/contact-service/contacts ← REST, not OData

Attempt 3: Live Connection

Sales Cloud V2 has embedded SAC capabilities. Could we establish a live connection?

The problem: The embedded analytics are designed for "embedded" use cases. Connecting a standalone SAC tenant to Sales Cloud V2 for custom reporting seems to be... let's say "not a focus area" right now.

I opened a support ticket to clarify the expected behavior and it was put in development after a little back and forth.

The Hidden Challenge: Real-Time vs. Import

At this point, I had to step back and discuss architecture with the customer.

Even if a direct connector existed, there are implications to consider:

 

ApproachProsCons
Live ConnectionAlways current dataQuery performance depends on source; cannot combine with other data sources easily
Import ConnectionFast queries; can blend with other sourcesData has latency; storage costs

The customer wanted to combine Sales Cloud V2 data with SAP S/4HANA data in the same dashboard. This essentially requires import connections for both sources, plus a data model in SAC that joins them.

Real-time was never really an option as they needed data at rest in SAP Analytics Cloud 

The Solution: A Lightweight REST-to-OData Proxy

Since the customer had:

  • No appetite for SAP Datasphere
  • No CPI license
  • A clear, limited scope (three entity types)
  • Tolerance for daily data refresh (not real-time)

I proposed building a thin proxy server that:

  1. Authenticates with Sales Cloud V2
  2. Fetches data via REST APIs
  3. Transforms responses to OData format
  4. Exposes endpoints that SAC can consume natively

Architecture

This sounds straightforward... until you start building it...

Challenge 1: The 999 vs. 1000 Mismatch

This one deserves its own section because it’s so unexpected.

SAP Sales Cloud V2 REST APIs have a maximum page size of 999 records. You can request $top=999, but not more.

SAP Analytics Cloud OData import has a minimum batch size of 1000 records.

Yes, you read that correctly. The source maxes out at 999. The target starts at 1000. I'll admit I stared at the logs for longer than I'd like to admit before I realized what was happening. The first 999 records imported perfectly. Then nothing. It took me a while to connect that SAC was waiting for a 1000-record batch that would never arrive.

The Solution

The proxy implements on-demand parallel fetching:

async function fetchAllRecords(entityType: string): Promise<any[]> {
  // Get first page to determine total count
  const firstPage = await fetchFromSalesCloud(entityType, {
    $top: 999,
    $skip: 0
  });
  
  if (firstPage.value.length < 999) {
    // All records fit in first page
    return firstPage.value;
  }
  
  // Calculate remaining pages and fetch in parallel
  const totalEstimate = firstPage.totalCount || 10000;
  const remainingPages = Math.ceil((totalEstimate - 999) / 999);
  
  const pagePromises = Array.from({ length: remainingPages }, (_, i) => 
    fetchFromSalesCloud(entityType, {
      $top: 999,
      $skip: 999 * (i + 1)
    })
  );
  
  const results = await Promise.all(pagePromises);
  return [firstPage.value, ...results.map(r => r.value)].flat();
}

The proxy fetches pages in parallel (not sequentially), caches the combined result, and serves it to SAC in appropriately sized batches. The cache has a configurable TTL (e.g., 1 hour) and automatically refreshes on the next request after expiry.

Challenge 2: Nested Objects and Flattening

Sales Cloud V2 returns nicely structured JSON with nested objects:

{
  "value": [{
    "accountId": "A001",
    "accountName": "ACME Corp",
    "primaryContact": {
      "contactId": "C001",
      "firstName": "John",
      "lastName": "Doe"
    },
    "addresses": [
      { "type": "BILL_TO", "city": "Berlin" },
      { "type": "SHIP_TO", "city": "Munich" }
    ]
  }]
}

SAC’s OData importer cannot handle this. The SAP Help documentation states clearly: Embedded Complex types are not supported.

The Solution

The proxy must flatten nested structures into separate entity sets:

/odata/Accounts         → Flat account records
/odata/Contacts         → Flat contact records  
/odata/AccountAddresses → One row per address, linked by accountId

Challenge 3: REST APIs Behave Differently Than OData

Coming from the OData world, I expected certain conventions:

  • $filter for filtering
  • $select for field selection
  • $expand for related entities
  • Standardized metadata endpoint

Sales Cloud V2 REST APIs have their own conventions:

  • Different query parameter syntax
  • No $metadata endpoint
  • Pagination via custom headers or response properties

The proxy needs to translate between these worlds, implementing just enough OData semantics to satisfy SAC’s importer.

Challenge 4: The $skip 10,000 Limit

Just when you think you’ve handled pagination, there’s another surprise waiting for datasets with more than 10,000 records.

Sales Cloud V2 returns an error if $skip is 10,000 or greater.

// This works
GET /accounts?$top=999&$skip=9000  ✅

// This fails
GET /accounts?$top=999&$skip=10000  ❌ Error!

So even if you’ve cleverly worked around the 999 page size limit, you hit a wall at record 10,001. Simple offset-based pagination ($skip) just stops working.

The Solution

Instead of offset-based pagination, the proxy uses keyset pagination with $skiptoken:

// src/constants.ts
export const SC_MAX_SKIP = 10000;

// When approaching the skip limit, switch to skiptoken
async function fetchWithKeysetPagination(entityType: string): Promise<any[]> {
  const allRecords: any[] = [];
  let nextLink: string | null = buildInitialUrl(entityType);
  
  while (nextLink) {
    const response = await fetch(nextLink);
    const data = await response.json();
    
    allRecords.push(...data.value);
    
    // Use the skiptoken from the response for next page
    nextLink = data['@odata.nextLink'] || null;
  }
  
  return allRecords;
}

Keyset pagination uses a token (typically based on the last record’s ID or timestamp) rather than a numeric offset. This approach:

  • Has no upper limit on the number of records
  • Is actually more performant than large offsets
  • Is the pattern SAP recommends for large datasets

If you have more than 10,000 records in any entity, you must use $skiptoken-based pagination. Simple $skip won’t cut it.

Implementation Notes

Technology Choice

I built the proxy with:

  • Node.js + Fastify: Lightweight, fast, excellent TypeScript support
  • On-demand caching: No scheduled jobs; cache fills dynamically on first request
  • Docker deployment: Runs on any container platform (I use Coolify on a VPS)

This is a production-ready setup. The proxy has been running reliably for the customer with no issues.

Smart Caching: On-Demand Parallel Fetching

Rather than running scheduled batch jobs, the proxy uses an on-demand approach:

  1. First page served instantly: When SAC requests data, the proxy immediately returns the first page (up to 999 records) from Sales Cloud V2
  2. Parallel fetching in background: While SAC processes the first page, the proxy fetches remaining pages in parallel
  3. Cache populated for subsequent requests: By the time SAC requests page 2, the data is already cached
async function handleODataRequest(req: Request): Promise<Response> {
  // Check cache first
  if (cache.has(entityType) && !cache.isExpired(entityType)) {
    return serveFromCache(entityType, req.query);
  }
  
  // Fetch first page immediately
  const firstPage = await fetchPage(entityType, 0, 999);
  
  // Start parallel fetching of remaining pages (non-blocking)
  fetchRemainingPagesInBackground(entityType, firstPage.totalCount);
  
  // Return first page immediately
  return formatAsOData(firstPage);
}

This approach has several advantages:

  • No unnecessary background jobs: Data is only fetched when actually needed
  • Fast initial response: User doesn’t wait for full dataset
  • Self-healing cache: Expired cache automatically refreshes on next request

Optional: Deploying to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry

If you already have a BTP subscription with available credits, deploying there can make sense. You’re using infrastructure you’re already paying for. The migration is straightforward:

Standalone Setup Cloud Foundry Setup

Environment variables for credentialsDestination Service binding
In-memory cachePostgreSQL Hyperscaler Option (free tier available)
Direct HTTP endpointXSUAA + AppRouter

Why PostgreSQL if you move to BTP?

On a single-container deployment (VPS e.g. with Coolify), in-memory caching works perfectly. But Cloud Foundry typically runs multiple app instances for high availability. If you need persistence across instances, PostgreSQL Hyperscaler Option is the lightweight choice. It has a free tier (since November 2025) and requires no additional management.

The additional effort for Cloud Foundry deployment is roughly:

  • Add mta.yaml with service bindings (~1 hour)
  • Configure Destination Service for Sales Cloud V2 credentials (~30 min)
  • Add XSUAA for authentication (~1 hour)
  • Test and deploy (~2 hours)

Total: Less than one additional day if you decide BTP is the right fit.

Don’t just default to BTP just because it’s SAP. Evaluate what you already have and what makes sense for your situation. A €5/month VPS running Docker might be exactly right for your use case.

OData Response Format

SAC expects OData V2or V4 responses. The minimum viable format:

{
  "d": {
    "results": [
      { "AccountID": "A001", "AccountName": "ACME Corp", ... },
      { "AccountID": "A002", "AccountName": "Globex", ... }
    ]
  }
}

Plus a $metadata endpoint returning EDMX that describes your entity types.

Authentication

Sales Cloud V2 supports:

  • Basic Authentication (simplest for server-to-server)
  • OAuth 2.0 (more secure, recommended for production)

The proxy stores credentials securely and handles token refresh for OAuth.

Results

After implementing the proxy:

  • SAC successfully imports Accounts, Contacts, and Opportunities
  • Data refreshes nightly via scheduled import
  • Customer can blend CX data with S/4HANA data in unified dashboards
  • No additional SAP platform licenses required
  • Total implementation time: ~2 days

When This Approach Makes Sense

The right solution depends on what you have and what you need. Here’s how I think about it:

A lightweight proxy makes sense when:

  • Limited scope (specific entities, not the entire data model)
  • Import/batch refresh is acceptable (not real-time requirements)
  • You don’t have Datasphere or CPI licensed and don’t need them for other use cases
  • You want to validate the integration quickly before investing in bigger infrastructure

Datasphere + CPI makes sense when:

  • You need event-driven, near real-time data replication
  • You’re integrating many entity types with complex relationships
  • You already have Datasphere or CPI licensed then use what you’re paying for
  • You have a broader data strategy where Datasphere is the central hub anyway

The key question: What do you already have, and does this use case justify adding more?

SAP’s platforms are excellent, no question! But sometimes a focused solution that solves exactly your problem is better than pulling in the big guns for a straightforward requirement.

Lessons Learned

1. Don’t Assume SAP Products Integrate Seamlessly

Sales Cloud V2 and SAC are both modern SAP cloud products. The integration gap exists because V2’s move away from OData wasn’t accompanied by a new native SAC connector (yet).

2. Check API Patterns Before Committing to Architecture

The shift from OData to REST in Sales Cloud V2 is documented, but easy to miss if you’re coming from C4C experience. Always verify on api.sap.com.

3. Test with Realistic Data Volumes

The 999/1000 mismatch only surfaces with more than 999 records. Demo systems with 50 accounts work fine. Production breaks.

4. Use What You Have, Validate What You Need

The official architecture is powerful. But if you don’t already have Datasphere and CPI licensed, ask yourself: does this specific use case justify adding them? Sometimes a focused solution that solves exactly your problem is the right call. You can always scale up later if requirements grow.

What’s Next?

I’ve published a reference implementation on GitHub that demonstrates the core patterns:

  • REST-to-OData transformation
  • Parallel fetching with caching
  • The 999→1000 batch bridging logic
  • Keyset pagination with $skiptoken for large datasets (>10k records)

👉 GitHub: SAP Sales Cloud V2 OData Proxy

A note on scope: The repository shows how the solution works, but doesn’t include everything from my production implementation. Some aspects are tied to specific client configurations that I can’t share. If you’re facing a similar challenge and want help getting to production faster, feel free to reach out.

If you’ve built something similar or found a better approach I’d love to hear about it in the comments.

entry.title: Systems not able to process messages via SAP Cloud Connector as of Q1/2026
entry.content:
Nowadays SAP Cloud Connector is a critical system for customers to process their integrations for their core business processes.

SAP has communicated some months ago about switching to the "DigiCert TLS RSA4096 Root G5" and “DigiCert Global Root G3” Public Key Infrastructures (PKI). This is only one of the many KBAs created around this topic KBA "3566727 - Root Certificate Replacement in the SAP BTP, Cloud Foundry Environment" 

Recently we have seen many customers reporting issues regarding Cloud Connector not processing messages.

In Cloud Integration this may be presented as an HTTP response '503: Service Unavailable.' The accompanying error message states: 'org.apache.cxf.interceptor.Fault: Could not send Message., cause: org.apache.cxf.transport.http.HTTPException: HTTP response '503: Service Unavailable. There is no SAP Cloud Connector (SCC) connected to your subaccount matching the requested tunnel for subaccount.'"

Customers get confused, because the Cloud Connector displays that the subaccount is connected.

On BTP Cockpit, they also see the Cloud Connector. 

However, the messages sent to be processed via this Cloud Connector still fail. 

How to find the cause?

Set the traces as per the following:

Reproduce the issue and download the "scc_core.trc" trace file. Then revert the trace settings as before.

Inside the file "scc_core.trc", look for string "PKIX path building failed: sun.security.provider.certpath.SunCertPathBuilderException: unable to find valid certification path to requested target."

For example:

Your issue is likely caused because of a missing certificate. Check this KBA "3583377 - Cloud Connector will fail to connect to subaccount"

The certificates come with the JVM, review and ensure you have at least the versions below:

  • SAP JVM 8.1.097
  • SapMachine 11.0.22 (SapMachine 11 is out of maintenance, though)
  • SapMachine 17.0.10
  • SapMachine 21.0.2
  • Oracle JDK 8u401

If your version is under the ones mentioned above, update as per this documentation https://help.sap.com/docs/connectivity/sap-btp-connectivity-cf/update-java-vm.

In case you have previously imported an own CA root certificate into the JVM keystore (for example a special certificate needed by their local LDAP system). Then review this approach mentioned by @Ulrich_Schmidt1. 

"What is interesting to know: there are even further problems, complicating this issue. If a user had previously imported an own CA root certificate into the JVM keystore (for example a special certificate needed by their local LDAP system), then the installer of the JVM apparently notices that the keystore has been modified and will not update it!

This means, even after the required version of the JVM has been installed, it can happen that the new DigiCert G5 certificate is still not available!

Fixing this can then get quite tricky: e.g.

  1. Make a fresh install of the JVM into a separate directory
  2. Reimport the "special" certificate into its keystore
  3. Change JAVA_HOME in the SCC's daemon to the new directory"

This should resolve your issue, if not, you may open an incident with BC-MID-SCC.

Best Regards,
Victor

entry.title: SAP BTP - IS - ( IA | CPI ) - The Commander's Blueprint: Rebuilding B2B EDI Governance with SAP BTP.
entry.content:
Spoiler

Disclaimer: This article is written for Integration Architects, Solution Architects, B2B Leads, Managers, and Integration Consultants dealing with B2B EDI modernization to SAP BTP Integration Suite. It focuses on governance, strategy, not in quick wins.

Instead, it is a strategic orientation on the mindset and professional positioning required of an SAP BTP Integration Suite Consultant (IA + CPI).

My goal is to provide a strategic guide on how to approach a scalable solution and lead a business transformation, rather than just explaining how to configure isolated components without considering the entire ecosystem.

Please note: This is not a "click-by-click" tutorial or a step-by-step technical manual.

Introduction

In my previous article, SAP BTP - IS - How to become a SAP Integration Suite Consultant - Part IV - I outlined a path for integration professionals to evolve beyond the “CPI Developer” label toward true SAP BTP Integration Suite specialization.

Today, I want to put this into practice by discussing a critical scenario: the modernization of B2B/EDI processes in S/4HANA projects.

Many companies adopting Rise with SAP and Clean Core methodologies still make the strategic mistake of maintaining legacy platforms (such as Seeburger or OpenText) or, even worse, they attempt to rebuild decades of EDI logic within massive and manual CPI iFlows. The result is what I call the "EDI-T-REX Legacy": a heavy, undocumented architecture that no one dares to touch.

SAP BTP Integration Advisor (IA) is the tool that breaks this cycle, transforming what would be a "Frankenstein" of code into clean governance, where the functional team regains control over what is being mapped.

My journey with Integration Advisor has been one of deep technical discovery. Until recently, it was an area I had not explored in depth — which makes the insights shared here the result of focused study and hands-on experimentation rather than inherited project patterns.

Quick Project Setup B2B EDI

Let me show you an imaginary project conversation.

The following imaginary conversation highlights a common mistake: treating SAP BTP Integration Advisor as an afterthought rather than the core of your EDI governance and structural framework.

Enterprise Architect / Solution Architect

Our next challenge: global B2B EDI migration with ~20 international partners across legacy platforms.

We're dealing with a chaotic mix of standards (EDIFACT, ANSI X12, VDA, RosettaNet), each with partner-specific deviations.

The real question: Are we migrating platforms or just moving decades of undocumented business semantics?

Will we implement a true semantic layer or replicate "partner-to-partner spaghetti" in a modern tool ?

Who actually owns these standards and partner lifecycle today in SAP BTP Integration Suite ?

SAP BTP Integration Suite Consultant - Commander 

OK I understand your worry, my proposal is rebuilding semantic governance, not just migrating EDI flows.

Using SAP Integration Advisor's MIG objects, we centralize business semantics. MAGs automated rules mapping transformation.

TPM shifts ownership from people to platform every contract becomes a managed asset for seamless partner onboarding.

Reality check: Integration Advisor exposes our weaknesses. 

Enterprise Architect / Solution Architect

Clear asnwer thank you but how do we avoid adapter hell (HTTPS, AS2, SFTP, OFTP2, X.400)?

Number range  collisions across partners, hidden Groovy "quick fixes" for encoding/delimiters, SAP API Management boundary: REST wrappers vs. batch mindset in case that is needed ?

BTP concerns: Handling Functional Acknowledgments (997/CONTRL) without doubling message volume.

Support teams already drown in dashboards APIM + TPM adds complexity.

Single Pane of monitoring ?

SAP BTP Integration Suite Consultant - Commander

Use SAP CPI use standard EDI Integration Templates and TPM V2—not custom iFlows.

Place APIM in front for quota management and spike control in case that is needed and only to partners that use API https or Soap.

SAP BTP manages ICN per partner, preventing collisions. Integration Advisor handles delimiters/encoding—no Groovy hacks. Status correlation links acknowledgments to original messages, avoiding dashboard clutter.

Monitoring: Application Correlation IDs track payloads and B2B and CPI monitoring centralized in B2B Monitoring in SAP CPI.

Legacy protocols (X.400, OFTP2): Third-party adapters only when needed. Maximize native Integration Suite for Clean Core architecture.

Enablement: I'll personally train the EDI ops team on MIGs, MAGs, and TPM.

Bottom line: Platform complexity handled. You focus on strategy; I'll deliver the engine and team.

Enterprise Architect / Solution Architect

Fair enough. That is exactly the level of technical clarity and leadership I was looking for to validate the viability of this project. You’ve addressed the architectural risks and, more importantly, the human element of this migration.

Phased approach: Start with 2 strategic vendors and 2 EDI formats to build our "Golden Template." This establishes confidence in SAP Integration Advisor (MIG, MAG, TPM) without overwhelming operations.

Your mission: Prepare the platform and enablement materials. Align the front-line team with your vision.

Let's start to think in possible remove the "T-Rex" and enter a new governance era. Time to execute.

Thank you for your support—let’s get to work.

The "T-Rex" Scenario: Why the fear of change is your greatest risk?

In the image below, I describe what I call the "Legacy Trap Mindset." It is the famous "T-Rex": an EDI ecosystem that no one wants to touch because "it has been working for 10 years."

The problem is that no one knows where the documentation is, how the transformations were built, or how to perform quick maintenance in case of an error. Within the context of Clean Core, keeping this dinosaur alive creates an innovation bottleneck.

Image 1 - trap mindste legacy for B2B EDI

A SAP BTP Integration Suite - IA Perspective: Where does Integration Advisor truly fit?

Although IA is underutilized in the market, the hands-on mastery I have acquired by studying this tool in depth allows me to face this chaos with a clear strategy. If I were leading this migration today, my stance as an SAP BTP Integration Suite Consultant would be structured on two pillars:

Enablement and Trust (The Human Factor)

I would provide a practical internal course to give confidence to the business team, B2B specialists, and SAP functional consultants. They need to understand that IA is not just another "IT tool," but a platform that returns mapping control to those who truly understand the business rules. It leverages Machine Learning and SAP’s Crowd Encyclopedia to accelerate what used to take weeks of manual coding.

Governance and Architecture (The Technical Factor)

This is where many fail: creating MIGs (Message Implementation Guidelines) and MAGs (Mapping Guidelines) in isolation only speeds up technical construction but doesn't solve the underlying disorder. My role would be:

  • Defining Semantics: Creating MIGs with real business meaning.
  • The "Canonical Path": Structuring the integration using a canonical model ( When two types of EDI message type are semantic with the respective message type from SAP side)
  • Materialization via TPM: Demonstrating how Trading Partner Management (TPM) must be used to materialize integration templates and contracts.

Without alignment between the contracts defined in TPM and the mappings in IA, the most valuable assets are lost: governance, traceability, and reusability. It is not about "copying and pasting" legacy logic, but using IA to clean the architecture—leaving SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) only with lightweight technical execution and lean iFlows.

“MIG and MAG build - TPM governs - Without TPM, there is no sustainable B2B integration.” 

Image 2 - SAP BTP Integration Suite Consultant - Internal Enablement on SAP Integration Advisor.

References
  • SAP BTP Integration Advisor Demo
  • How to build custom B2B Interface using Integration Advisor , AS2 Adapters and API Proxy
  • Integration Advisor: Overview of components for building B2B integration content and further reading

A SAP BTP Integration Suite - IA Perspective: fit into MIGs?

The first step in killing the "T-Rex" is the correct construction of MIGs (Message Implementation Guidelines). As a consultant, my role is to guide the team at the base of the pyramid:

  • 1:1 Structure: Creating one object per Message Type (EDI or SAP), ensuring granularity.
  • Business Semantics: Going beyond the technical side by defining rules, field usage, and documenting Codelists.
  • Facilitation: Acting as the bridge that translates technical standards (EDIFACT/X12) into the actual requirements of the SAP process.

Responsibilities and Roles of the SAP BTP Integration Suite Consultant - IA - MIG

What the IA Consultant DOES:

  • Facilitation: Teaches how to operate the IA and apply the correct semantics to fields.
  • Technical Oversight: Ensures the MIG follows the standard (1:1) and remains technically reusable.
  • Mentorship: Guides the documentation of codelists and qualifiers.

What the IA Consultant DOES NOT do:

  • Define Business Rules: The business decides what it needs; the consultant only shows where to implement it.
  • Data Governance: The consultant delivers the structure, but responsibility for the data itself lies with the process owner.
  • Flow Decisions: Does not interpret commercial rules without validation from Key Users.

“MIG defines clarity, semantics, and standards."

Image 3 - SAP BTP Integration Suite Consultant - Providing guidance on where and how to create a MIG and select the message type.

References - MIG
  • Creating MIGs and MAGs Using Integration Advisor in SAP BTP Cockpit
  • integration content advisor: Create a customized interface using MIG editor
  • Integration Advisor – multi-payload support for MIG creation
  • Integration Advisor – direct support of EDI payloads
  • Creating MIGs and MAGs Using Integration Advisor in SAP BTP Cockpit
  • integration content advisor: Discover B2B/A2A standard libraries
  • Integration Advisor : Use of cXML Type System Exporting to CPI

A SAP BTP Integration Suite - IA Perspective: fit into MAG?

If the MIG is the definition of the object, the MAG (Mapping Guideline) is where the transformation magic happens. The consultant acts as the guide who materializes this connection.

What the Consultant DOES:

  • Connectivity: Guides the correct selection of Source and Target MIGs.
  • Targeted Mapping: Explains how to perform field-to-field mapping, ensuring that the semantics defined in the MIG are respected.
  • Conversion Logic: Implements and teaches how to use basic conversion functions to adjust data between standards (e.g., transforming date formats or units of measure).
  • Artifact Generation: Demonstrates how to generate the schemas (XSD) and transformations (XSLT) that the CPI will utilize.

What the Consultant DOES NOT do:

  • Define Complex Business Logic: The consultant implements the technical conversion, but calculation rules or commercial decisions must come from the functional side.
  • Map Without a Base: The consultant must not start a MAG if the MIGs have not been properly validated by the business.

“The MAG is not a technical mapping; it is an adapter between meaning and the platform.”

Image 4 - SAP BTP Integration Suite Consultant - How to create a MAG from MIG outbound and MIG Inbound

References MAG
  • Integration Advisor: complex mapping code for dimension conversion in SAP B2B
  • Base-/Overlay Mappings for Integration Advisor
  • Integration Advisor – Download MAG Simulation Result as EDI Payload
  • Accessing Headers and Properties in Integration Advisor (IA) Mapping Guidelines (MAG) with the Global Parameters
  • Integration Advisor: Conditional Mapping Feature
  • Integration Advisor: Conditional mapping in MAG to pass one value from the multiple occurrence of an element, based on other element's value
  • Complex Mappings 
    • SAP MAG mapping many occurrence node to 1 occurrence node and take last occurrence values to target
    • SAP TPM MAG or IA MAG :choose or multiple if conditions
    • SAP TPM MAG conditional mapping target node it's child

Where does an SAP BTP Integration Suite Consultant fit into TPM?

The SAP BTP Integration Suite Consultant must guide and enforce the use of TPM (Trading Partner Management) as a true governance layer, to be honest, TPM is not a "ten-headed beast" or some unreachable diamond; however, the most critical part is the definition of Identifiers within the EDI envelopes. If these are configured incorrectly, the agreement will fail to trigger even if everything else is perfect. I will demonstrate this below.

What is NOT the responsibility of the (IA/TPM) Consultant

For a migration to be sustainable, the consultant must not take over tasks that belong to business management or day-to-day operations.

The Consultant DOES NOT:

  • Define Commercial Rules: Does not decide legal terms, freight conditions, or SLAs. The business defines the rule; the consultant only configures it.
  • Operational Execution (TPM): Is not responsible for the continuous registration of hundreds of partners, certificate maintenance, or agreement versioning once the project has stabilized.
  • Contract Negotiation: Does not act in the alignment of deadlines or contractual obligations with external suppliers and customers.

Summary: The consultant designs the architecture and the migration process. The operational life of the tool (onboarding new partners and business decisions) must be managed by the company's internal EDI specialist team.

“MIG defines the meaning, MAG defines the destination, TPM defines the contract.”

Image 5 - SAP BTP Integration Suite Consultant - How to create a TPM

References
  • TPM Onboarding Demo
  • Use TPM, IA, CI to Efficiently Manage and Run Complex B2B Transactions
  • Bridging EDI and SAP S/4HANA Cloud: Seamless EDI-to-SOAP Integration

The Developer’s Playground: Where SAP CPI Experts Take Control

Now, we enter the territory where we, as SAP CPI experts, have total dominance.

Once all the steps above are completed—specifically once the TPM is configured and the Agreement is accepted and deployed—something critical happens behind the scenes.

We aren't just "connecting pipes" anymore; we are orchestrating a dynamic, metadata-driven engine.

Image 6 - SAP BTP Integration Suite Consultant - CPI Implementation

Technical Deep Dive: Standard Packages and the "Plain ID" Trap

I will now explain the possibilities and the path I took to streamline this integration. I am intentionally ignoring the option of building a fully manual, custom iFlow. Such an approach would be unnecessarily complex, time-consuming, and redundant given the standard packages available.

Standard EDI Package Options:

EDI Integration Templates for SAP Integration Advisor - A versatile set of iFlows for various scenarios.

Cloud Integration - Trading Partner Management V2- The complete governance suite.

The TPM V2 Workflow:

  • Step 1: Ingress (Generic, depends on the channel: HTTP, SFTP, SOAP, IDoc, ProcessDirect, AS2).
  • Step 1b: Persistency (Write Message to Message Queue).
  • Step 2: Interchange Processing Flow V2 (The "Brain").
  • Step 3: Receiver Communication Flow V2.

My Assessment: After debugging every step in both packages, the standalone EDI Integration Templates work perfectly if you have your MIGs and MAGs ready, standalone iFlow is only valid for small-scale integrations.

Image 6 - Inject Process MAG to SAP CPI Iflow

The "Isolation Test" Strategy: Validating the MAG

Pro-Tip: I used the standalone iFlow to validate my mapping (MAG) before moving to TPM, injecting the MAG directly into a copy of the standard iFlow and testing with a Content Modifier using plain text (X12 850), I could validate the logic instantly. This is crucial because if you try to debug the Step 2 - Interchange Processing Flow without a prior test, grab a coffee and bring plenty of patience and papers because you are going to cry as me.

Crucial Recommendation: Pay close attention to the Validate and Analyze EDI Interchange step configuration. I suggest mirroring the settings in Step 2 - Interchange Processing Flow V2 exactly.

How to do it:

  • Configure the Pre and Post-processing steps with your generated XSD and XSLT files.
  • Run a test using a Content Modifier with the raw EDI "N" format (in my case, an X12 850) as plain text.
  • It doesn’t matter if it’s EDI to IDoc or, as I tested, EDI to OData—the communication channel is secondary at this stage.

Note on Testing: Please keep in mind that I am an expert in integration, not in business semantics or specific EDI file structures. Many of my test files were generated manually or extracted from internet, which caused significant issues during the validation phase.

Image 7 - Validacao da MAG e verificado do EDI Splitter

The "Agreement Plain ID" Headache

Once everything is configured and the agreement is deployed in TPM, it generates a unique "Information Package" for the trading partner based on identifiers. This is where the Agreement Plain ID comes in the configuration of identifiers in the Company and Partners regards the exchange header validation.

Imagem 8 - Aggremment Active in TPM

After successfully completing these steps, you will generate the "Trading Partner Information Package." This process creates a unique identification string based on the company's and the partner's identifiers.

This object—the Agreement Plain ID—is exactly what caused me so much frustration during the implementation.

What does a string like SOAP-ASC-X12--004010-BUYERCO-ZZ----SAP-ZZ----850 actually mean? It is a concatenation of the identifiers found in the EDI header:

  • SOAP: Partner Sender Channel
  • ASC-X12--004010: Message Type
  • BUYERCO-ZZ: Partner Identifier
  • SAP-ZZ: Receiver Identifier
  • 850: Transaction Code (Orders)

The Trap: If these identifiers are registered incorrectly in Company and Partner sides —even by one character—the integration will fail. There is a Groovy script of nearly 1,000 lines inside the standard package that handles this logic. I had to debug it to understand why my agreement wasn't being found.

Forced error: ISA*00*...*ZZ*BUYERCO*ZZ*SAP2... (The "SAP2" mismatch prevents the iFlow from finding the Agreement in the TPM table).

 
Imagem 9 - Bug between Partner Agremment ID and the header detail of the message as EDI or SAP Message Type

Crucial: Number Range Synchronization Ensure the Number Range is identically named in the TPM Company/Partner, the Agreement, and the SAP CPI Number Range monitor. Failure to align these exactly will trigger errors. I also experienced a platform bug where a locked Agreement prevented the deletion/update of the INC_UN_EDIFACT range.

 

Image 10 - Number Range

Pro Tip: Custom Number Ranges If a TPM Agreement gets stuck (e.g., locking INC_INB_EDIFACT), use a Custom Number Range. This bypasses platform bugs and is a best practice for high-volume scenarios to prevent duplicate sequence numbers across different partner agreements, remember that this was test with ODETTE EDIFACT 96A not with another scenario X12.

In the platform that I have access there is somehow to agreements that was not delete 100% locking the INC_INB_EDIFACT, I have no idea how to delete or if must open SAP ticket for it:

 

Image 11 - Trading Agrement locking the INC_INB_EDIFACT.
SAP CPI Iflow - Step 2 - Interchange Processing Flow V2 - for SAP TPM - SAP Integration Advisor and always failed in this step:

 

Image 12 - Set Control Numers fails

Error: javax.script.ScriptException: java.lang.Exception: java.lang.IllegalStateException: Error reading number range ICN_UN_EDIFACT @ line 46 in setControlNumbers.groovy, cause: java.lang.IllegalStateException: Error reading number range ICN_UN_EDIFACT

I debugged another simple iFlow and it worked, so I still couldn't understand the cause of these locked records. I opened a thread on SAP SCN but received no response. I then tested a workaround—which I do not recommend—that involved modifying the standard CPI iFlow in Step 2, though I later reverted it to the original version.

Image 13 - Workaround Iflow to check outside TPM config the number range ICN_UN_EDIFACT 

Image 13 - Workaround Iflow to check outside TPM lock range number INC_UNB_EDIFACT

Inside the Step 2 - Interchange Processing Flow, there is a Local Integration Process for mapping. Since MAGs are based on XSLT and have some limitations, you can use ProcessDirect to call PRE and POST processing flows for complex logic or fine-tuning.

Image 14 - Local Integration Process - PRO and POS mapping processing

Now, let’s look at the "perfect scenario"—everything built, configured, and ready for action. In this case, I decided to push the limits: I used Scenario 1 with a SOAP call executing an EDI Flat File via a SOAP envelope.

The result? Don't worry about Step 5; I am simply looping the message back into the CPI for processing.

Imagem 15 - Full processing
 

Stop Guessing, Start Proving

You don’t have to replace everything at once. The most viable path is a low-risk POC (Proof of Concept). Pick a single, non-critical flow, apply the Integration Advisor methodology, and witness the efficiency firsthand. Once the value is proven, you can execute a robust, confident migration.

Industrialized Automation vs. Manual Labor

The large-scale EDI migrations, tools like int4 Shield (formerly IFTT) and Figaf automate what would be manual labor.

Key benefits:

  • Extract legacy definitions → auto-convert to TPM entries and iFlows
  • Months of development → weeks of configuration
  • Capture real messages → run through new system → automatic comparison
  • Ensures 100% mapping fidelity without partner involvement

Result: Migration becomes governance and audit, not error-prone manual work. Predictable timelines, lean team, no supply chain disruptions.

  • Int4 Shield (formerly IFTT):
    • Int4 Shield - Migration to SAP Integration Suite
    • SAP News - Test Automation for SAP Integration Suite
    • SAP Store - Advanced Business Process Testing
  • Figaf Tool:
    •  Figaf - SAP EDI Migration Tooling
    • Figaf Docs - Connecting to Integration Advisor
    • Figaf Blog - Migrating EDI to SAP Integration Suite

 Image 16 - The Beginning of the T-Rex Extinction 

Conclusion

Modernizing EDI on SAP BTP isn't just moving iFlows from on-premise or cloud it's remove the control from outside to insade with governance and structure.

Using Integration Advisor as the semantic brain and CPI as the agile engine, we transition from technical workarounds to managing true business assets.

Creating strategic content connecting architectural vision with technical execution takes weeks of deep work. If this "Commander's Blueprint" helped you see beyond standard tutorials.

Drop a comment. Share your perspective. LET'S GO!

Best regards,

SAP BTP Integration Suite Expert - Viana

entry.title: Jan 28th 2026 - Join Our Live Session: Starting with SAP Integration Suite for Customer Experience
entry.content:
On behalf of the SAP Product learning team, I am thrilled to invite you to an exciting live session where we’ll dive deep into the SAP Integration Suite capabilities for SAP Customer Experience solutions.

Session Details

📅 Date: 28 January 2026 ⏰ Time: 15;:00 hrs CET 🎤 Speaker: Florian Kupke

What You’ll Learn

Join SAP experts for a practical, hands-on live session. Whether you’re responsible for connecting SAP and non‑SAP systems, streamlining customer journeys, or accelerating CX projects, this session will show how SAP Integration Suite helps you integrate data, automate processes, and deliver seamless customer experiences. 

  • Why integration is critical for modern CX and common integration patterns for CX scenarios
  • Core capabilities of SAP Integration Suite relevant to Customer Experience (connectivity, adapters, APIs, integration flows)
  • A guided demo showing how to set up a simple integration for a CX use case
  • Best practices for designing secure, scalable, and maintainable integrations
  • Next steps and resources to continue learning

Why Attend?

  • Learn directly from practitioners and see real-world examples
  • Reduce time-to-value by adopting proven integration patterns and practices
  • Ask questions during the live session and get targeted guidance
  • Access links and materials to accelerate your hands-on learning after the event

How to Join

Click here to register!

Don’t miss this opportunity to see how getting started with SAP Integration Suite for Customer Experience. Learn integration patterns, see a demo, and ask questions live.

See you there!

entry.title: How to Configure an API Proxy: From Provider Setup to Testing
entry.content:
Introduction :  Application Programming Interface which allows to communicate with SAP systems for different software applications. Simply they let you read ,update , create records in SAP.  Also helps you to Integrate SAP with Non SAP systems via cloud services or webapps.

We have 3 types of API's 

1.SOAP

2.REST

3.ODATA

In the below steps you'll know how to create API's and assign the target endpoints , resources respectively

Step 1: Create API Proxy

  • Go to Configure and create a new API Proxy.
  • Select the respective API Provider that has already been created.
    Note: If the provider does not exist, create a new API Provider using the credentials provided.

 

Step 2: Configure Provider Details

  • Select the provider and enter the Target URL.
  • Fill in all required details using the proper naming conventions (API name, version, description, etc.).

 

Step 3: Select Service Type

  • Choose the appropriate service type (e.g., REST, SOAP, ODATA).
  •  Click on create  , the API proxy will be created.

 

Step 4: Configure Target Endpoint (Routing Conditions) If routing conditions are required:

    • Navigate to the Target Endpoint.
    • Add the required routing rules/conditions.

Step 5: Configure Proxy Endpoint

  • Go to the Proxy Endpoint.
  • Add the conditions.
  • Map the condition to the Target Endpoint created in Step 4.

 

Step 6: Policies and Deployment

  • Save the configuration.
  • Apply the required policies (security, logging, transformation, etc.).
  • Deploy the API Proxy to the required environment.

Step 7: Add Resources (If Required)

  • If resources are not created automatically:
    • Download the YAML file from API Hub.
    • Import it into API Designer.

Note:

  • For most REST ,ODATA, SOAP APIs, resources are created automatically.
  • If not, resources can be added manually and tested.

 

Step 8: Authorization and Parameters (GET / POST)

For GET and POST operations, ensure the following parameters are added after parameters section:

for GET you need to add after parameters section where for POST need to add after tag which means we need to add the parameters as well for POST operation

- $ref: '#/components/parameters/authorization'
- $ref: '#/components/parameters/legalEntityId'

Step 9: Testing

  • Test the API using Postman.
  • Pass the routing name value in the legalEntityId parameter in headers of postman.

Summary : The vital steps to setup basic API its types , creation of an API  and assigning the target endpoints setting up the routing conditions accordingly and assign the resources manually using YAML file and deploying it.

entry.title: Custom Integration Flows for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition integration with SAP IBP
entry.content:
Hello Community!

SAP has provided several standard integration flows in SAP Integration Suite (Cloud Integration) - such as Products, Plants and others as part of the SAP IBP - Integration with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Package, documented here. 

However, when additional data needs to be integrated with SAP IBP, custom integration flows must be developed.

This blog walks through the process of create a simple, custom integration flow in SAP Integration suite to integrate data with SAP IBP.  As an example, it demonstrates how to integrate Work Center Header from S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to SAP Integrated Business Planning using SAP Integration Suite.

Prerequisites

  • You have activated the required business processes and set up the necessary communication arrangements in your SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition system.
  • You have access to an SAP BTP Global Account and subaccount with an active subscription to SAP Integration Suite, including the Build Integration Scenarios capability.
  • You have preconfigured the SAP IBP – Reusable Integration Flows within SAP Cloud Integration.
  • You possess a basic understanding of SAP Integration Suite and its components used to develop integration flows.
  • You have the username & password to access S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition

Detailed guide regarding setting up the necessary prerequisites is documented in SAP Help Portal.

Reusable Integration Flows

As described in the prerequisites, deploy the reusable preconfigured integration package SAP IBP - Reusable Integration Flow from Discover - Integrations Section as shown below.

Configuring Security Material

Before connecting to S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition, a security material needs to be created in SAP Integration Suite which will be used by the adapters to communicate. You can configure the security material using the following steps.

  • Go to Monitor → Integrations & APIs
  • Under Manage Security, click on the Security Material tile

  • Click on Create to create a new security material. We will be configuring the username, password to connect to the S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition.
  • Select User Credentials and provide the following details.
ParameterPurpose
NameThe name with which the security material will be referenced. The value provided here will be referenced in the integration flows.
Description 
UserUsername of the instance
PasswordPassword to authenticate the user

Procedure

Identification of the necessary API

Before you can build the integration flow, the necessary API and it’s details will have to be identified.

  • Navigate to the Business Accelerator Hub.
  • Click on the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition as your product.
  • Click on APIs tab. Here you will see a list of packages, and APIs provided by SAP for interacting with S/4HANA Public.
  • Open the APIs tab, where you’ll find a list of API packages and services provided by SAP for interacting with the S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition system.
  • Since our objective is to integrate Work Center Header, we will be using the corresponding ODATA V4 API, specifically the GET Work Center Header operation to retrieve all relevant entries.
    • API Reference: API Reference | Work Center | SAP Business Accelerator Hub

Creating the Integration Flow

SAP recommended naming conventions for artifacts developed using Integration Suite can be found here. They can act as a starting point to your development in SAP Integration Suite.

  • Navigate to Design → Integrations and APIs → Create a new Package.
  • Create a new integration flow artifact inside the package.
  • Click on Add and Open in Editor to open the GUI editor.

Building the integration flow

The integration flow is broken down into three, Local Integration Processes. They are created using Process → Local Integration Process.

The following are the local integration processes will be built as we progress with the blog.

 

Local Integration Process NamePurpose
Transfer Data to IBPThis process is responsible to extract data from the S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition API (OData) and load into IBP 
Create Batch in IBPBefore loading the actual data in IBP, a batch (data integration job) needs to be created
Post Processing in IBPThis job is responsible to trigger the IBP Post Processing (processing data from staging tables to the application tables) once the actual data load is complete.

These Local Integration Processes are then sequenced in a Main Integration Process which will trigger the subsequent process sequentially.

Transfer Data to IBP

  • Create a Local Integration Process
  • Add an External Call → Request Reply and name it Extract Data from S/4
  • Add a Participant → Receiver and name it S4HANA.
  • Connect the Request Reply with Receiver. Use the OData V4 adapter with the following connection configuration.
Address

<S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition URL> :443/sap/opu/odata4/sap/api_work_center/srvd_a2x/sap/workcenter/0001

AuthenticationBasic
Credential NameName of the Security Material (created as part of the prerequisites)
CSRF ProtectedUncheck

  • Click on the Select button near the Resource Path. Use Remote connection source and ensure that the details are configured as per previous step.

  • Click on Step 2 button
  • Based on the API identified earlier, select the following.
OperationQuery (GET)
Select EntityWorkCenterHeader

 

Select only the necessary fields (columns) required for the integration as this will avoid unnecessary data fetch from the API.

 

Spoiler
You may also be able to filter necessary data in this step, but in this example, we are sending all the records from S/4HANA Public Cloud to IBP.

 

  • Click on the Finish button.
  • Add a new Mapping → XSLT Mapping and connect the request reply to the XSLT mapping.
  • In your local device, create a new WorkCenterMapping.xsl file with the following code.
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<xsl:stylesheet version="3.0"
    xmlns:xsl="http://www.w3.org/1999/XSL/Transform">
    <xsl:output method="xml" indent="yes" encoding="UTF-8" />

    <!-- Root template -->
    <xsl:template match="/">
        <IBPWriteMasterData
            MasterDataPrefix="A01"
            FieldList="RESID,RESDESCR"
            MasterDataType="RESOURCE"
            FileName="RESOURCE"
            BatchKey="WorkCenter">
            <xsl:for-each select="WorkCenterHeader/WorkCenterHeaderType">
                <item>
                    <RESID>
                        <xsl:value-of select="WorkCenterInternalID" />
                    </RESID>
                    <RESDESCR>
                        <xsl:value-of select="WorkCenterDesc" />
                     </RESDESCR>
                </item>
            </xsl:for-each>
        </IBPWriteMasterData>
    </xsl:template>
</xsl:stylesheet>

In the above example, ensure to remember the value provided for BatchKey as this will be used in the other integration processes.

  • Upload the XSL file in the XSLT Mapping Component.
  • Add an External Call → Request Reply and name it Write data to IBP
  • Add a Participant → Receiver and name it as IBP_Write_Data.
  • Connect the Request Reply with the Receiver with ProcessDirect adapter and with the connection address as SAP_IBP_Write_-_Post_Data.

Finally, the local integration process should like as follows.

Creating a batch in IBP

  • Create another local integration process and name it as Create IBP Batch.
  • Add Transformation → Content Modifier.
  • Paste the following in the Message Body of the Content Modifier.
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8" standalone="no"?>
<IBPWriteBatches>
    <IBPWriteBatch  
        Key="WorkCenter" 
        Name="Load Resource from S/4HANA Public Cloud - Integration Suite"
        Destination="<IBP System Name>"
        Command="INSERT_UPDATE"
        PlanningArea="<Planning_Area>"
        PlanningAreaVersion=""
        DetailedTrace=""
        ProcessUnchangedData="FALSE"
      />
</IBPWriteBatches>

Ensure to provide the Destination with your IBP system name and the correct planning area in the above code.

  • Add an External Call → Request Reply and name it Create Batch in IBP
  • Add a Participant → Receiver and name it as IBP_Create_Data.
  • Connect the Request Reply with the Receiver with ProcessDirect adapter and with the connection address as SAP_IBP_Write_-_Create_Batches.

Finally, the local integration process should like as follows.

Post Processing in IBP

  • Create another local integration process and name it as Post Processing in IBP.
  • Add Transformation → Content Modifier.
  • Paste the following in the Message Body of the Content Modifier.
<IBPWriteBatches>
	<IBPWriteBatch 
           Command="INSERT_UPDATE"
           Destination="<IBP System Name>"
           Id="WorkCenter" 
           Index="1" 
           Key="WorkCenterInternalID" 
           Name="Load Resource from S/4HANA Public Cloud - Integration Suite" 
           PlanningArea="<Planning Area>"  
           ProcessUnchangedData="True"/>
</IBPWriteBatches>

 Provide the correct Destination and Planning Area. Also note that we have provided value of the Id attribute as WorkCenter, which is the BatchKey that we had defined earlier in our XSLT mapping. These values need to match, otherwise the data will not be loaded into IBP.

  • Add an External Call → Request Reply and name it IBP Post Processing Call.
  • Add a Participant → Receiver and name it as IBP_Post_Processing.
  • Connect the Request Reply with the Receiver with ProcessDirect adapter and with the connection address as SAP_IBP_Write_-_Process_Posted_Data.

The local integration process should look as follows.

Main Integration Process

Since our local integration processes are now created, we can trigger them sequentially. The Main Integration Process is already created when the integration flow was created, with the name Integration Process.

  • Remove the existing Start Message and add a timer component
  • Click on Call → Local Call.
  • Perform this three times and sequence the components one after the other.
  • In the first local call, select the Create IBP Batch local integration process (under Processing tab)
  • In the second, select Transfer Data to IBP
  • In the third, select Post Process in IBP.

Your main integration process should look as follows.

Conclusion

The final integration flow should look as follows.

 

  • Once the integration flow is built, click on Save and then Deploy the integration flow.
  • Since we are using a timer event here, the integration flow will be executed as soon as it is deployed.
  • You can log into IBP and check the Data Integration Jobs app to see the status of the integration performed by the Integration Flow.

 

 

Using this approach, you may load any kind of data with some minor tweaks in the mappings (XSLT mapping, message body for batches and post processing).

Sources:

  • SAP Documentation on IBP Reusable Integration Flows
  • IBP Reusable Integration Flows in SAP Business Accelerator Hub

entry.title: Real-Time XML to EDI Conversion: A Step-by-Step CPI Integration Scenario 2025
entry.content:
Hello all, welcome to this blog on SAP Cloud Integration and EDI. Business partners in many B2B projects still exchange data in classic EDI formats, while modern applications speak XML or JSON. Bridging these formats reliably is one of the common challenges for integration developers working with SAP CPI.

In this article, we will go through a full, hands-on example of receiving an XML message over HTTPS, converting it to EDI, enriching it with a Groovy script, and finally delivering it to the receiver via email. The aim is to provide you with a clear, end‑to‑end view of how to design this flow, which components to use, and what configuration details matter in real projects.

The Scenario we are working is shown below.

FULL SCENARIO IMAGE

 

 Before Going Into the Scenario we need to understand about the EDI ,where it is used and all that .

So,EDI is the automated, system‑to‑system exchange of business documents in a standard electronic format, as opposed to paper or email and manual data entry. It is also widely used in B2B processes like orders, invoices, shipping notices, and inventory updates across retail, manufacturing, logistics, automotive, and healthcare. The main standards are UN/EDIFACT (global), ANSI X12 (North America), plus others like TRADACOMS and healthcare‑focused HL7, each with defined message types such as ORDERS/INVOIC (EDIFACT) or 850/810 (X12).

>For the Trail Account We have only option to use Orders(EDIFACT) Standard only.

Now we will go step by step process : -

1)Create an iflow first and from the sender connect to the start event as show in the figure and select the HTTPS adater to send the XML data from the Postman. Here I'm using postman to send the data , we can also use the timer Event with content modifier to start the iflow.

 2)After connecting with the HTTPS adapter, Deply the iflow so we need can get the HTTPS endpoint as shown below, so we can use that endpoint in postman to post the data to cpi.

POstman page

 

ENDPOINTS

 3)Now Before going to use XML to EDI converter we need to use EDI xsd's to use for this conversion.So now we need to go to the Settings-->MIGs and MAGs --> click on the trails as shown in the image below to activate the three standards.

Activate these three standards

 Accept the each standard like this

 

After Activating looks like this

 

4)After that we need to go to the Design-->MIGs-->click on Create option as shown in the image below.

5)After that we need to select the type system that we are using in xml to EDI converter , select the sysstem shown in the image below.

CLICK ON UN/EDIFACT

 6)After that we need to select the ORDERS standard because for the trail accounts we can only  select the ORDERS standard froom the list ,so search for it until you find it .

SEARCH FOR ORDER

7)After Finding it click on ORDERS to  proceed to the next steps  as shown below.
Click on the ORDERS to proceed

 

8)Select version "D.96A s3" as shown below. This is best version for trail accounts.

Select this version

 9)After the version selection we need to select the Envelope we need have in our system. Use the 3rd Envelope as shown in the image to proceed to the next steps.

Select the Envelopes

 10)After the above step we  need to SKIP the sample payload step and proceed to the MIG creation step.

SKIP the sample payload step

 

11)Here we need to Enter the Name of the MIG and Direction its work , we are selecting it as BOTH , so it will work as IN and OUT Directions.

Enter the MIG name and Direction

 

12) After that , Click on the plus icon and select the 'Bussiness Process' and after that we have access the select the list of business processess we have ,so from the drop down select all 250 processess and click on CREATE to create the MIG.

Select all process

 13) After creation , created MIG will be appear here , in my case i have created and exported so it is not showing here. For you guys it shown here and when we have that MIG , click on and open that MIG , on the top right side we have three dots icon click on it and EXPORT the MIG as 'SAP cloud integration runtime artifacts' , so that one ZIP file will be downloaded and we can seen it our laptop downloads folder . Extract it to get the both xsd files one is normal EDI xsd and another is EDI xsd for runtime. Most of the times we use only one file i.e "UN-EDIFACT_ORDERS_D96A.xsd" .

 

CREATED MIG SHOWN HERE

 

EDI xsds in exported folder

 14) Drag a XML to EDI converter step from the pallate and add to the iflow as shown in the first image of full iflow starting of the Blog. Click on the EDIFACT and inside that change connections as shown in image and remaining all default and also add the EDI xsd file by clicking on the add button as shown in the image from your laptop import it here. we dont have to change any thing for X12 and Tradacoms.

 

EDIFACT connection page

 

15) After that I'm using  Groovy script to Log the payload and see the output . Also we can see the output via MAIL also. so, Drag a groovy script and attach it after xml to edi converter .

GROOVY SCRIPT IM USING  :-

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message

def Message processData(Message message) {
def body = message.getBody(String)

// Log to Message Processing Log (MPL)
def messageLog = messageLogFactory.getMessageLog(message)
if (messageLog != null) {
messageLog.addAttachmentAsString("EDI_OUTPUT", body, "application/xml")
}

// Ensure Content-Type is XML
def headers = message.getHeaders()
headers.put("Content-Type", "application/xml")
message.setHeaders(headers)

// Return the mapped XML
message.setBody(body)
return message
}

16) After this from the End message connect mail adapter to the receiver and give the details as shown in the images . And also we need to create "Security material" for the mail we are using for sending mails . I think you guys known how to create it so that Im not including it .

PROCESSING PAGE

 

CONNECTION PAGE

 

SECURITY MATERIAL OVERVIEW

 

 17) After all these steps we need to deploy the iflow and send the input payload from the postman and we get the output in groovy and MAIL . Select the option in poastman as shown image below and send it.

INPUT payload  for postman :-

-->For payload see below data.xml file uploaded or here is the code :-

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<Interchange>
	<S_UNA>:+.? '</S_UNA>
	<S_UNB>
		<C_S001>
			<D_0001>UNOC</D_0001>
			<D_0002>3</D_0002>
		</C_S001>
		<C_S002>
			<D_0004>SENDERABC</D_0004>
			<D_0007>14</D_0007>
			<D_0008>XXXX</D_0008>
		</C_S002>
		<C_S003>
			<D_0010>ReceiverXYZ</D_0010>
			<D_0007>14</D_0007>
			<D_0014>YYYYY</D_0014>
		</C_S003>
		<C_S004>
			<D_0017>150831</D_0017>
			<D_0019>1530</D_0019>
		</C_S004>
		<D_0020>1</D_0020>
		<C_S005>
			<D_0022>HELLO WORLD</D_0022>
		</C_S005>
		<D_0029>A</D_0029>
		<D_0035>1</D_0035>
	</S_UNB>
	<!-- First ORDERS message -->
	<M_ORDERS>
		<S_UNH>
			<D_0062>1</D_0062>
			<C_S009>
				<D_0065>ORDERS</D_0065>
				<D_0052>D</D_0052>
				<D_0054>96A</D_0054>
				<D_0051>UN</D_0051>
			</C_S009>
			<C_S010>
				<D_0070>2</D_0070>
			</C_S010>
		</S_UNH>
		<S_BGM>
			<C_C002>
				<D_1001>220</D_1001>
			</C_C002>
			<D_1004>ORDER1</D_1004>
			<D_1225>9</D_1225>
			<D_4343>NA</D_4343>
		</S_BGM>
		<!-- Further order details here -->
		<S_UNT>
			<D_0074>5</D_0074>
			<D_0062>1</D_0062>
		</S_UNT>
	</M_ORDERS>
	<!-- Second ORDERS message -->
	<M_ORDERS>
		<S_UNH>
			<D_0062>2</D_0062>
			<C_S009>
				<D_0065>ORDERS</D_0065>
				<D_0052>D</D_0052>
				<D_0054>96A</D_0054>
				<D_0051>UN</D_0051>
			</C_S009>
			<C_S010>
				<D_0070>2</D_0070>
			</C_S010>
		</S_UNH>
		<S_BGM>
			<C_C002>
				<D_1001>220</D_1001>
			</C_C002>
			<D_1004>ORDER2</D_1004>
			<D_1225>9</D_1225>
			<D_4343>NA</D_4343>
		</S_BGM>
		<!-- Further order details here -->
		<S_UNT>
			<D_0074>6</D_0074>
			<D_0062>2</D_0062>
		</S_UNT>
	</M_ORDERS>
	<S_UNZ>
		<D_0036>2</D_0036>
		<D_0020>1</D_0020>
	</S_UNZ>
</Interchange>



 
POSTMAN PAGE

 

OUTPUT IMAGES :-
 
GROOVY OUTPUT

 

MAIL OUTPUT

 

 

Thank you very much for taking the time to read this blog and walk with me through the XML-to-EDI scenario. Interest from people like you in topics like SAP Cloud Integration and EDI drives content like this, and I hope it helps in your real projects.

If you try out this flow, improve it, or run into interesting challenges, please share your experience and questions in the comments so others in the community can learn as well.

Happy integrating, and see you in the next blog!

entry.title: Event Enabling Idocs rapidly and almost effortlessly via BTP Integration Suite, Cloud Integration
entry.content:
1) Introduction

In the past we have published a couple of Blogs where we describe how to event enable Idocs via Event Enablement AddOn or Application Interface Framework. Another possibility with the least configuration on the ERP (ECC or S/4) System is the usage of the HTTP Port and SAP Cloud Integration. This Blog will demonstrate how to easily accomplish this + how to dynamically add Topic Values with the Help of the Partner Directory in SAP Cloud Integration.  

 

2) ERP Customizing

Let´s start with the Definition of the Logical System in Transaction SALE

I have defined this Logical System for the Connection to AEM via Cloud Integration

In SM59 I have already prepared a Destination to SAP Cloud Integration Iflow receiving the Idocs:

Of course later we´ll have to make sure that the Iflow Address and the endpoint in this Destination are the same. Based on this Destination we create a Port in WE21:

 

Then we´ll need a Partner Profile in WE20 with the Receiver Port we just created. Let´s say we want to distribute Vendor Master Data with CREMAS:

Also we´ll need a Distribution Model in BD64:

(Only the Receiver CPI_AEM relevant here)

That is all we have to do on the ERP! Let´s switch to the Cloud Integration.

 

2) SAP Cloud Integration

Static Topic

In my Github Repo I have provided a Template Iflow you can upload into your Cloud Integration Package 

First thing is to configure the Sender Address and Role:

 

In the Content Modifier Step we retrieve the Idoc Headers from the Payload:

Then, depending if the Idoc has a CIMTYP or not, we put together the Topic. 

With CIMTYP:

Header "Topic" = "${header.SAP_IDoc_EDIDC_SNDPRN}/${header.SAP_IDoc_EDIDC_MESTYP}/${header.SAP_IDoc_EDIDC_IDOCTYP}/${header.SAP_IDoc_EDIDC_CIMTYP}"

Without CIMTYP the Topic is filled like this: "${header.SAP_IDoc_EDIDC_SNDPRN}/${header.SAP_IDoc_EDIDC_MESTYP}/${header.SAP_IDoc_EDIDC_IDOCTYP}"

So if I have an CREMAS07 Idoc from my System T33, the Topic would be 

T33CLNT800/CREMAS/CREMAS07

If this is already enough for you, you can ignore or even remove the 2 Groovy Scripts after the Router in the Iflow.

The only Thing left of course is the Receiver Channel. Here we have to maintain the Connection Parameters

That´s it. You can deploy the Iflow!

Dynamic Topic with PartnerDirectory

If we want to include Parts of the Payload into the Topic, we´ll have to get it via Xpath. This could be easily done via Content Modifier, but to keep everything dynamic in one Iflow, I decided to implement 2 Groovy Scripts. 1st Script is retrieving the XPaths I set up for my Idoc Type in the Partner Directory. 2nd Script performs the XPath on the Body and puts together the Topic.
